data.title: Is Ventas (VTR) Still Attractive After A 35% One-Year Share Price Jump?
data.description:
• Wondering whether Ventas at around US$84.42 is offering fair value or if you might be overpaying for future growth? This article explains what the current price could be indicating about the stock.
  • The stock is up 9.2% year to date and 34.9% over the past year, even though it has slipped 4.3% over the last week and 3.4% over the past month. Over 3 years it is up 105.4% and over 5 years it has gained 76.2%.
  • Recent coverage around Ventas has focused on its position in the Health Care REITs space and broader interest in income focused real estate investments. Combined with its recent share price moves, this has kept attention on whether the current valuation still makes sense for new and existing shareholders.
  • Right now Ventas holds a value score of 3/6. This suggests some checks point to potential undervaluation while others do not. The rest of this article will look at standard valuation methods and then finish with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Ventas delivered 34.9% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Health Care REITs industry.

Approach 1: Ventas Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a stock could be worth by projecting its future adjusted funds from operations and discounting those cash flows back to today in dollar terms.

For Ventas, the latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.62b. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates for several years, and these are then extended further using the same 2 stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on adjusted funds from operations. On this basis, projected free cash flow for 2030 is $2.35b, with intermediate years between 2026 and 2035 ranging from about $1.55b to $3.06b in nominal terms, before discounting.

When all those future cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $110.94 per share. Compared with the current share price of about $84.42, this implies Ventas is trading at a 23.9% discount to this DCF estimate, which indicates the stock is undervalued according to this method.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Ventas Price vs Sales

For profitable companies, price-based multiples are a straightforward way to see what investors are paying for each unit of business performance. For Ventas, the preferred measure is the Price to Sales, or P/S, ratio, which compares the stock price to the revenue the company generates.

Expectations for future growth and the risk around those expectations usually drive what looks like a normal or fair multiple. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/S ratio, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple.

Ventas currently trades on a P/S of 6.71x, compared with the Health Care REITs industry average of 6.70x and a peer group average of 7.41x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio of 5.63x for Ventas. This Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it folds in factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, industry and overall market cap.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 5.63x with the current P/S of 6.71x suggests the stock is trading above that tailored fair estimate.

The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €74.2 million in Q1 2025 to €41.0 million in Q1 2026, while trailing twelve month net income sits at €59.8 million against €42.5 million two years ago. This gives helpful context for how the latest quarter fits into the recent earnings path. With trailing net profit margins currently well above last year’s level, this update gives investors a clearer read on how much of that profitability is holding up in the current run rate.

47.9% net margin versus shrinking forecasts

  • Over the last 12 months, Ernst Russ earned €59.8 million on €124.9 million of revenue, which works out to a 47.9% net profit margin compared with 26.6% a year earlier.
  • Bears point to forecasts that earnings could decline by about 29.6% per year over the next three years, yet
    • current margins of 47.9% and trailing earnings growth of 12.3% over the last year sit against that expectation of lower future profits and margins moving toward 11.9% in three years, creating a clear gap between recent profitability and the cautious view, and

  • For bullish investors, the combination of low multiples and high reported profitability heavily supports the idea of a valuation gap, yet
    • the same data set includes forecasts for earnings to decline by 29.6% per year over three years, which challenges the assumption that current margins and returns will persist, and
    • the DCF fair value of €73.04 and an analyst price target of €13.23 both sit above the current €9.10 share price, which bulls cite as support while bears focus on the earnings and margin compression embedded in those models.
On top of these headline ratios, bulls argue that long term charter contracts and a high equity ratio could help support cash flows even if earnings forecasts prove conservative, so it can be useful to compare this earnings print with the full bullish case in 🐂 Ernst Russ Bull Case.

  • Critics highlight these quality concerns as key to the bearish argument, because

    • the unstable dividend description sits awkwardly alongside the strong five year earnings growth record, prompting questions from cautious investors about how consistently these profits have supported shareholder returns.

data.title: Whale Rock Backs MKS As AI Infrastructure Supply Chain Attracts Attention
data.description:
• Whale Rock Capital Management has opened a substantial new position in NasdaqGS:MKSI.
  • The move reflects a shift in its portfolio toward suppliers of AI infrastructure.
  • This stake highlights fresh institutional attention on companies that support AI hardware and manufacturing.

NasdaqGS:MKSI is a supplier of equipment and technologies used in advanced electronics manufacturing, including areas tied to AI data centers and high performance computing. With more capital flowing into AI infrastructure, attention is widening from headline AI stocks to companies that provide the tools and components behind them. Whale Rock's new position places MKS within that group of suppliers.

For investors, this new holding can be a data point when assessing how professional money is treating the AI supply chain. It adds an angle to the MKS story that sits alongside valuation and share price behavior, and it may prompt a closer look at how the business is positioned in AI related end markets.

Whale Rock Capital Management’s new position in MKS puts a well-followed hedge fund on the same side of the AI-infrastructure trade that many retail investors are watching. Whale Rock has shifted capital away from Nvidia toward suppliers such as MKS, Advanced Energy Industries and Viavi Solutions, a sign that some institutional investors are looking further down the supply chain to companies providing tools, power systems and testing equipment for AI data centers and high-performance computing. For you, the signal is less about one fund’s view on MKS and more about where sophisticated capital is currently hunting for exposure to AI hardware buildout. However, a fresh hedge fund position does not remove company specific issues such as cyclicality in semiconductor equipment, leverage or competitive pressure from peers like Applied Materials, Lam Research or Entegris.   • The specific signal that a large hedge fund is rotating from headline AI stocks into infrastructure suppliers is not fully captured in the narrative, which mainly centers on end market demand and company execution.

data.title: Constellation Energy Extends Clean Portfolio With Pine Creek Renewable Gas Bet
data.description:
• Constellation Energy (NasdaqGS:CEG) has entered a long-term agreement with Pine Creek RNG.
  • The company is acquiring a minority equity interest in five renewable natural gas production facilities.
  • The move marks an expansion of Constellation Energy's clean energy portfolio into renewable natural gas.

Constellation Energy, trading at a recent share price of $287.75, is primarily known for its nuclear and renewables portfolio. The stock has seen a very large gain over 3 years, while being down 21.4% year to date and down 5.5% over the past year. This new renewable natural gas exposure adds another clean energy source to the mix at a time when the stock has pulled back from earlier highs.

The long-term agreement with Pine Creek RNG gives Constellation Energy direct involvement in renewable natural gas production assets. Investors following NasdaqGS:CEG may watch how this new segment fits alongside the existing generation portfolio and how it affects earnings and customer offerings over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Constellation Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Constellation Energy.

We've flagged 3 risks for Constellation Energy. See which could impact your investment.

This move into renewable natural gas gives Constellation Energy another way to serve large customers that want lower-carbon energy options alongside nuclear and renewables. Because Constellation is only taking a minority equity stake in the Pine Creek RNG assets, it gains exposure to this part of the value chain without taking on full project risk. For investors, the key questions are how meaningful this segment could become relative to the existing fleet and whether it supports Constellation’s efforts to win or retain long-duration contracts with corporates and data center operators. The agreement also slightly broadens the company’s fuel mix at a time when peers such as NextEra Energy, Duke Energy and Dominion Energy are also adjusting portfolios in response to customer decarbonization targets and regulatory scrutiny. Execution will matter, including how reliably these facilities operate, how contract structures are set up and whether Constellation can build on this initial step with further projects or partnerships over time.

How This Fits Into The Constellation Energy Narrative

  • The Pine Creek RNG agreement aligns with the narrative focus on long-term, higher-margin contracts for carbon-free and lower-carbon power. It may add another product that can be bundled into 24/7 carbon-free offerings.
  • The move slightly challenges the narrative’s emphasis on nuclear and centralized assets by introducing a different type of asset. This could complicate capital allocation and operational priorities if not managed carefully.
  • Renewable natural gas as a distinct product set is not a major feature of the existing narrative, so the potential impact on customer mix, margins and earnings volatility may not be fully reflected yet.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Constellation Energy to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Investors already face three flagged risks for Constellation Energy, including balance sheet and dilution concerns, and this new RNG exposure adds execution risk around a relatively new asset class.
  • ⚠️ If renewable natural gas policy support or credit markets weaken, returns from these facilities could be lower than expected, especially given Constellation’s minority position.
  • 🎁 The agreement can support Constellation’s positioning as a provider of cleaner energy solutions to large customers that want diversified low-carbon options, which may strengthen contract discussions.
  • 🎁 Adding RNG to a portfolio that includes nuclear, gas and solar could modestly diversify earnings sources and reduce reliance on any single technology or regulatory framework.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how Constellation reports contributions from the Pine Creek RNG assets, including volumes sold, contract structures and any commentary on customer demand for renewable natural gas. It is also worth tracking whether management references RNG more often when discussing long-duration contracts with data centers and corporates, which would signal growing commercial relevance. Finally, compare Constellation’s progress in low-carbon offerings against utilities such as NextEra Energy, Duke Energy and Dominion Energy to see whether this step helps the company stay competitive in serving large buyers with decarbonization goals.

data.title: Expanded Airbus Safety Directive And What It May Mean For Investors

• European and US regulators have expanded an airworthiness directive affecting Airbus A318 to A321 aircraft.
  • The directive requires mandatory inspections and replacement of main landing gear door actuators tied to potential landing gear malfunction and evacuation risks.
  • The scope now covers additional aircraft and serial numbers following updated safety investigations, impacting a significant portion of the Airbus single aisle fleet.

For investors following Airbus, ticker ENXTPA:AIR, this development touches the company’s core narrow body portfolio, which is widely used on short and medium haul routes by airlines globally. Airworthiness directives are a routine part of commercial aviation, but when they focus on key systems such as landing gear, they can influence maintenance planning, aircraft availability, and airline relationships.

The expanded directive introduces additional inspection and replacement work that airlines and maintenance providers will need to schedule. This can feed into operational decisions and cost planning. Investors may want to watch how Airbus addresses technical findings with regulators and customers, and whether any follow on design, production, or support measures are announced in response.

data.description:

  • The stock has seen sharp recent moves, with returns of 19.0% over 7 days, 61.0% over 30 days, 11.8% year to date, 94.1% over 1 year, 48.4% over 3 years, and 304.5% over 5 years. This naturally raises questions about how much is already reflected in the price.

Taken together, these factors help explain why many investors are reassessing both the potential and the risks embedded in the current share price.
  • First Solar currently has a valuation score of 3 out of 6. The sections that follow will walk through how common valuation approaches line up with that score, before finishing with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Find out why First Solar's 94.1% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: First Solar Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a stock could be worth by projecting future cash flows and then discounting them back to today, so you can compare that value with the current share price.

For First Solar, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The company’s last twelve months Free Cash Flow is about $1.01b. Analyst based projections and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St point to Free Cash Flow of $2.95b in 2030, with detailed estimates and extensions running from 2026 through 2035 in the model.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the estimated intrinsic value from this DCF is about $247.93 per share. The current share price is around $306.79, which implies the stock is trading at roughly a 23.7% premium to the DCF estimate. Based on this method alone, the valuation appears to lean to the expensive side.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for First Solar.

Approach 2: First Solar Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to current profitability, which most investors watch closely.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings appear. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

First Solar currently trades on a P/E of 19.80x. That sits below the broader Semiconductor industry average of 66.86x and well below the peer average of 114.07x. To go a step further, Simply Wall St calculates a “Fair Ratio” for First Solar of 40.37x. This proprietary metric aims to capture what P/E might be reasonable after considering factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, market cap, risk characteristics and its industry.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all stocks deserve the same multiple. Comparing First Solar’s actual P/E of 19.80x with the Fair Ratio of 40.37x suggests the stock screens as undervalued on this measure.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives let you see and build these stories yourself. They link a clear written thesis to a forecast model that updates automatically when new earnings, policy news or guidance is released, so your fair value view stays current rather than frozen in time.

Another might focus on U.S. manufacturing expansion, assumed revenue growth of 18.80% and a Fair Value closer to US$313.00. By setting out these different Narratives side by side, you can quickly see which story and fair value aligns best with your own assumptions before deciding how the stock fits your plan.

🐂 First Solar Bull Case

Revenue growth assumption: 18.80% a year.

  • U.S. manufacturing build out, Section 45X credits and a non Chinese supply chain are expected to support premium margins and earnings compared with consensus analyst views.

  • The Narrative still flags heavy reliance on government policy, technology execution and a concentrated U.S. footprint as key risks that could pressure demand, pricing and earnings if conditions change.

Kohl’s continues to drop despite a relatively positive reported earnings and balance sheet. The stock is now trading around 65% below the price it was at just a year ago. Total market capitalization has fallen solidly below $1 Billion, despite nearly $8 Billion in real estate assets and long-term debt of just over $1.1 Billion. The company, which continues to report sizable free cash flows and declining debt, is now effectively priced as though the market expects it to go bankrupt.
